---
title: |
  Machine Learning for Characterization and Processing<br>Unit 1: Why Materials Signals Challenge ML
bibliography: ref.bib
author:
  - name: Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz
    affiliation:
      - FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg
execute:
  eval: false
format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    css: custom.css
    theme: custom.scss
    slide-number: c/t
    logo: "eclipse_logo_small.png"
    footer: "© Philipp Pelz - ML for Characterization and Processing"
---


## 01. Title + course positioning

- ML-PC focuses on experimental signals and processing data under real lab constraints.
- Unit 1 builds trustworthy pipeline habits before advanced models.

## 02. Why experimental materials data is uniquely hard

- Noise, drift, artifacts, and sparse labels break textbook assumptions.
- Modeling must be co-designed with measurement physics.
::: aside
@ryan2021machine
:::

## 03. Unit 1 learning outcomes

- Classify modalities and pitfalls, build a leakage-safe baseline, and justify decisions.
- Differentiate confidence from correctness in lab settings.

## 04. Data modalities in this course

- Images, spectra, time series, and process logs require different preprocessing and metrics.
- Multimodal fusion introduces alignment and calibration issues.

## 05. What students bring from prerequisites

- You already know core math; now emphasis is failure analysis and trustworthy evaluation.
- Coding level assumed basic; labs scaffold implementation.

## 06. How this course uses MFML foundations

- MFML objective/risk notation will be reused directly in this course.
- This course adds domain-specific constraints and deployment realism.

## 07. 90-minute lecture roadmap

- Modality physics -> task mapping -> validation -> uncertainty -> exercise.
- We prioritize reliability over architectural novelty.

## 08. Initial discussion prompt (failure story)

- Start with a real failure pattern to motivate pipeline discipline.
- Ask: where did assumption break first?

## 09. Micrographs: signal sources + artifacts

- Contrast mechanisms and sample prep affect labels and feature stability.
- Artifacts can become spurious shortcuts if uncontrolled.

## 10. Spectra: resolution/noise/background effects

- Resolution, baseline drift, and denoising choices alter downstream labels.
- Keep preprocessing auditable and reversible when possible.

## 11. Process logs: temporal correlation and drift

- Temporal dependence invalidates random split assumptions.
- Lag features and grouped validation are often mandatory.

## 12. Label quality and annotation variance

- Annotation uncertainty and inter-rater disagreement limit attainable performance.
- Track label confidence and disagreement statistics.

## 13. Instrument dependence and domain shift

- Domain shift across instruments can dominate model error.
- Use source-aware splits and adaptation checks.

## 14. Calibration and uncertainty metadata

- Measurement uncertainty metadata is valuable supervisory context.
- Uncertainty-aware models can avoid brittle thresholds.

## 15. Heteroscedastic noise in experiments

- Error variance changes across regimes; one-size losses may mislead.
- Consider weighted losses or stratified diagnostics.

## 16. Why IID assumptions often break

- Many lab datasets violate IID due to batch/process correlations.
- Validation design must mirror deployment flow.

## 17. Physics priors as regularization intuition

- Physics priors act as inductive bias and regularization.
- They improve plausibility and often reduce data demand.
::: aside
@neuer2024machine
:::

## 18. Mini-case: same sample, different instrument bias

- Compare two instruments on same sample to show shortcut-learning risk.
- Use as argument for domain-robust evaluation.

## 19. Checkpoint question

- Short concept check on assumptions and split design.
- Target written-exam style argumentation.

## 20. PSPP graph recap (processing→structure→property→performance)

- Map ML tasks to PSPP edges to avoid vague problem definitions.
- Edge-specific metrics and costs should be explicit.
::: aside
@sandfeld_materials_data_science
:::

## 21. Task mapping onto PSPP edges

- Each edge implies different observables, delays, and uncertainty profiles.
- State what is controllable versus merely observable.

## 22. Regression tasks in process modeling

- Examples: process parameters -> hardness, grain size, porosity metrics.
- Report uncertainty, not only point predictions.

## 23. Classification tasks in defect detection

- Examples: defect/no-defect, phase labels, quality classes.
- Handle imbalance and asymmetric misclassification costs.

## 24. Structured outputs (segmentation, spectra decomposition)

- Resolution, baseline drift, and denoising choices alter downstream labels.
- Keep preprocessing auditable and reversible when possible.

## 25. Baseline-before-complexity principle

- Start simple to reveal data and split issues quickly.
- Complex models should justify added complexity with robustness gains.

## 26. Objective functions linked to deployment goals

- Loss must align with deployment utility and physics tolerance.
- Do not optimize proxy metrics without decision analysis.

## 27. Model selection under limited labels

- Select models with validation protocols reflecting operational setting.
- Hyperparameter search can overfit if protocol is weak.

## 28. Cross-validation with grouped/temporal splits

- Use grouped/temporal CV for correlated experimental data.
- k-fold random CV is often optimistic in labs.
::: aside
@ryan2021machine
:::

## 29. Leakage examples specific to lab data

- Common leaks: replicated samples, preprocessing on full dataset, temporal look-ahead.
- Detect leaks via strict pipeline encapsulation.

## 30. Metrics and cost asymmetry

- False negatives and false positives have different lab costs.
- Choose thresholds using decision costs, not default 0.5.

## 31. Recap of pipeline skeleton

- Define question -> split -> baseline -> evaluate -> diagnose -> iterate.
- Document each stage for reproducibility.

## 32. Generalization under domain shift

- Define the concept with one concrete engineering/materials example.
- State one failure mode and one validation criterion for this concept.

## 33. Overfitting patterns in small lab datasets

- Small data + strong models easily memorize artifacts.
- Use learning curves and ablations to detect this early.
::: aside
@murphy2012machine
:::

## 34. Bias–variance in experimental ML practice

- Control model capacity and data augmentation based on error decomposition.
- Bias-variance language helps debugging strategy.

## 35. Aleatoric vs epistemic uncertainty (engineering interpretation)

- Aleatoric: measurement/process noise; epistemic: model ignorance.
- Treat epistemic reduction as an experimental design target.

## 36. Calibration concept for classification

- Probability outputs must reflect empirical frequencies.
- Calibrated models support safer thresholding and triage.

## 37. Uncertainty-aware decision thresholds

- Thresholds should be chosen from utility/cost perspective.
- Consider reject option for uncertain cases.

## 38. When to reject predictions

- Abstention can reduce harm in high-uncertainty regimes.
- Design fallback workflows for rejected predictions.

## 39. Explainability expectations by stakeholder

- Technicians, scientists, and managers need different explanation depth.
- Choose explainability tools matched to stakeholder question.
::: aside
@neuer2024machine
:::

## 40. Failure analysis template for lab projects

- Classify failures by data, model, evaluation, and deployment mismatch.
- Turn findings into corrective action items.

## 41. Reproducibility checklist (data, code, split, metrics)

- Version datasets, splits, code, and environment.
- Reproducibility protects against accidental performance inflation.

## 42. Scientific claim checklist

- Require uncertainty bounds and ablation-based causal claims.
- Avoid overstating generality from narrow datasets.

## 43. Checkpoint MCQ slide

- Short concept check on assumptions and split design.
- Target written-exam style argumentation.

## 44. Exercise objective: baseline on real-ish materials data

- Build a trustworthy baseline on a small experimental dataset.
- Success = defendable methodology, not maximal score.

## 45. Step 1: clean + split with leakage control

- Data cleaning + leakage-safe split design.
- Document assumptions and potential confounders.

## 46. Step 2: baseline model and metric selection

- Train baseline model and compute core metrics.
- Inspect subgroup performance by source/instrument.

## 47. Step 3: error analysis by subgroup/source

- Perform structured error analysis and failure categorization.
- Relate errors to modality physics.

## 48. Step 4: add one physics-aware feature/constraint

- Add one physics-aware feature or constraint.
- Re-evaluate to test whether gain is robust.

## 49. Unit summary: 10 must-know statements

- Capture 10 high-yield exam statements.
- Tie each statement to one practical example.

## 50. References + reading assignment

- ML in engineering should be motivated by **physical-system questions**, not benchmark-only framing (McClarren preface + Ch. 1).
- Data-science success in materials requires **domain knowledge integration**, not isolated algorithm use (Sandfeld Ch. 2.1).
- Explainability and traceability concerns are valid in technical environments and must be addressed explicitly (Neuer Ch. 1.1.2–1.1.3).
- **Lecture**: modality physics, problem framing, evaluation validity, uncertainty and trust.
- **Exercise**: implement baseline, compare split strategies, diagnose failures, add one domain-aware improvement.
- McClarren Ch. 1.1–1.5
- Neuer Ch. 1.1 + 1.3 (CRISP-DM perspective)
- Murphy Ch. 1.4 (overfitting/model selection)
::: aside
@neuer2024machine; @sandfeld_materials_data_science; @ryan2021machine; @murphy2012machine; @bishop2006pattern
:::

::: {#refs}
:::
